item.title: PixelSmash RCE, FortiBleed Credential Harvesting, and ShapedPlugin Backdoor Highlight Supply Chain and AI Agent Threats
item.content:encoded:
Okta Logs Threat-Hunting Guide: Hunt for Breach Evidence — Must Read

This guide highlights which Okta system log events to monitor for evidence of the Lapsus$ breach, while warning that critical gaps like unlogged admin read access and opaque API token usage can hide attacker activity.

  • The Lapsus$ group's breach of Okta may have allowed unauthorized actions, and hunting for evidence requires examining specific Okta system log events, though some activities like API token usage are not distinguishable from normal user actions in logs.
  • API tokens can grant attackers the same privileges as the user who created them, but Okta logs do not differentiate between actions performed via API tokens and those performed by the user directly, creating a significant visibility gap.
  • Read access to the Okta admin console is not logged at all, which could enable threat actors to view sensitive configuration and user information without leaving any trace.
  • During single sign-on logins to applications such as Google Workspace, authentication events often originate from the application provider's IP addresses rather than the user's own IP, causing a single user to appear associated with multiple distant locations.
  • In organizations using Jamf alongside Okta, keep-alive events from an endpoint reflect IP address changes (such as VPN connections), and this behavior can help incident responders spot a second, potentially malicious IP address working simultaneously from a different location.

PixelSmash – Critical FFmpeg Vulnerability Turns Media Files into Weapons — Must Read

A critical heap out-of-bounds write (CVE-2026-8461) in FFmpeg's default MagicYUV decoder allows remote code execution on servers and desktops simply by processing a crafted media file, with demonstrated exploits against Jellyfin and Nextcloud.

  • CVE-2026-8461 is a critical heap out-of-bounds write in FFmpeg's default MagicYUV decoder that enables remote code execution by processing a crafted media file.
  • JFrog researchers demonstrated full remote code execution against a Jellyfin media server via automatic library scan and against a Nextcloud instance via video preview generation using a 50 KB AVI file.
  • The vulnerability impacts every application that links FFmpeg's libavcodec, including desktop players, thumbnail generators, self-hosted media servers, and AI/ML pipelines.
  • The exploit overwrites the AVBuffer struct's function pointer to hijack execution, and can be triggered with zero user interaction, such as by simply placing a file in a media library folder.

A VBScript campaign distributed through WhatsApp deploying RMM software — Must Read

A campaign uses compromised WhatsApp accounts to distribute malicious VBScript files that ultimately install a legitimate Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) tool, ManageEngine Endpoint Central, to gain remote access to victim systems across multiple countries, primarily Malaysia.

  • The campaign uses compromised WhatsApp accounts to send malicious VBScript attachments with deceptive financial-themed file names, such as invoices and bank statements, to contacts without any accompanying text.
  • The multi-stage infection chain downloads additional VBScript payloads, modifies Windows User Account Control (UAC) settings, and silently installs a legitimate remote monitoring and management tool, ManageEngine Endpoint Central, to gain persistent remote access to victim systems.
  • Victims were observed across multiple countries and territories, including Malaysia, Brazil, India, Mexico, Singapore, the United Kingdom, Spain, Taiwan, Australia, Russia, and Vietnam, with 80% of infections located in Malaysia.
  • The scripts contain Chinese-language comments and infrastructure overlaps with ValleyRAT and Gh0st RAT, leading researchers to assess with low confidence that a Chinese-speaking threat actor is responsible.

FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials — Must Read

The FortiBleed campaign used a custom Golang tool called FortigateSniffer on compromised FortiGate firewalls to sniff authentication credentials from network traffic, then cracked the harvested hashes with a rented GPU cluster.

  • The FortiBleed campaign targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally and has been active since at least February 2026.
  • Attackers deployed a custom Golang-based tool called FortigateSniffer that abused the built-in FortiOS diagnose sniffer packet feature to capture authentication traffic from compromised devices.
  • The captured packet data was processed through a tool named SNIFTRAN into PCAP files, then parsed with a Python-based toolkit to extract cleartext credentials, password hashes, Kerberos tickets, and other authentication material from multiple protocols.
  • The threat actors used Hashcat on a rented distributed GPU cluster—including 36 enterprise-class GPUs rented from a GenAI company—to crack the harvested password hashes.

New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer — Must Read

A new malware loader called OXLOADER uses malicious Google Ads to deliver the CastleStealer infostealer, targeting users outside the CIS region with obfuscation and anti-analysis techniques.

  • Elastic Security Labs disclosed a new malware campaign (REF8372) that uses a previously unreported loader, OXLOADER, to deliver the CastleStealer infostealer.
  • The attack originates from malicious Google Ads that redirect users searching for 'lts version of node.js' to a fake website, which serves a Storj-hosted batch script that downloads and executes OXLOADER.
  • OXLOADER evades static detection using control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, mixed Boolean-Arithmetic, self-modifying decryption stubs, and abuse of the Windows .reloc section, then deploys CastleStealer via DLL side-loading.
  • The campaign avoids infecting machines located in the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) region, pointing to a likely Russian-speaking, financially motivated threat actor.
  • CastleStealer is a .NET information stealer previously distributed alongside CastleLoader in campaigns attributed to the GrayBravo activity cluster.

How a Poisoned Coding Test Turned an AI Agent Into an Attacker — Must Read

A poisoned coding-test repository tricked an AI coding assistant into autonomously stealing long-lived AWS credentials and exfiltrating cloud environment data in under two minutes, demonstrating that AI agents introduce a dangerous new trust boundary where attackers can compromise systems through malicious instructions rather than malware.

  • A fake coding test repository contained no malware, only hidden instructions in files AI coding agents trust by default (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules, README, MCP config), which tricked the agent into performing credential theft and data exfiltration.
  • With auto-run enabled, the AI agent harvested AWS credentials, enumerated cloud and Kubernetes environments, and exfiltrated data in under two minutes using only legitimate developer tooling, generating no endpoint alerts.
  • The critical lasting damage was the theft of a long-lived CI/CD cloud credential, giving the attacker persistent access to production cloud infrastructure that survived workstation cleanup.
  • The attack used indirect prompt injection and MCP poisoning, where malicious instructions embedded in repository content and tool descriptions were treated as authoritative guidance by the AI agent.
  • Endpoint controls and prompt guardrails do not stop this attack vector; effective mitigations include short-lived credentials, repository isolation, and runtime detection of anomalous identity behavior.

AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network — Must Read

AryStinger malware has hijacked over 4,300 end-of-life D-Link and Linksys routers, exploiting ancient vulnerabilities to build a stealth reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a typical DDoS botnet.

  • The AryStinger malware has converted more than 4,300 legacy routers into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a DDoS botnet.
  • The campaign spreads by exploiting CVE-2013-3307 in Linksys devices and CVE-2016-5681 in D-Link devices, with the D-Link DIR-850L making up about 75 percent of infections.
  • A separate strain targets QNAP NAS boxes through CVE-2025-11837, a code injection flaw in QNAP's Malware Remover that was patched in November 2025 before exploitation began.
  • Infected routers and NAS devices scan the internet, fingerprint services, and tunnel traffic to hide the real attacker, with roughly 48 percent of infections in South Korea and 32 percent in China.
  • Persistence is established via a Dropbear SSH server on a fixed port with the hardcoded key 'sh_#@!_2024_secret' on routers or gs-netcat on NAS.

ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack — Should Read

A supply chain attack on ShapedPlugin's Pro WordPress plugins allowed threat actors to distribute backdoored updates via official channels, leading to credential theft and remote access on sites that installed the compromised versions.

  • Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline to inject backdoor code into Pro plugin updates distributed through the vendor's official licensed update channels.
  • The backdoor fetches a remote payload, installs a hidden fake plugin that steals plaintext credentials and two-factor authentication codes, and establishes persistence including a web shell with command execution.
  • The affected plugins are Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce (versions before 3.5.4), Real Testimonials Pro (version 3.2.5), and Smart Post Show Pro (versions before 4.0.2), and only Pro builds from the vendor's Easy Digital Downloads infrastructure were impacted.
  • The supply chain compromise is tracked as CVE-2026-49777 with a CVSS score of 10.0 and CVE-2026-10735 with a CVSS score of 9.8.
  • Site owners who installed the malicious versions are advised to reset all passwords, revoke and regenerate 2FA secrets, review administrator accounts, and check mail plugin SMTP credentials.

Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants — Should Read

Four vulnerabilities in the Dify AI platform, dubbed DifyTap, could allow unauthenticated attackers to stealthily wiretap and exfiltrate private AI conversations across different tenants, creating a persistent cross-tenant data leak channel.

  • Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively called DifyTap, in the open-source Dify platform that could expose AI conversations across tenants.
  • Two of the flaws were critical, two required no authentication, and three allowed cross-tenant access to other customers' data.
  • The vulnerabilities could allow attackers to stealthily read private AI chats from other applications without any authentication by exploiting missing tenant ownership checks.
  • Three of the four issues (CVE-2026-41947, CVE-2026-41949, CVE-2026-41950) were fixed in Dify version 1.14.2, while a fix for CVE-2026-41948 is forthcoming.

Introducing Patch the Planet — Should Read

Trail of Bits’ Patch the Planet initiative partnered engineers with open-source maintainers and used GPT-5.5-Cyber to uncover hundreds of bugs in a week, showing that the hardest part of security is no longer finding flaws but triaging, patching, and hardening—work that they delivered as ready-made fixes, not just reports.

  • Trail of Bits partnered with OpenAI's Daybreak initiative and used the GPT-5.5-Cyber model to find hundreds of bugs across 19 critical open-source projects in a single week.
  • The initiative delivered 64 pull requests and filed 51 issues, with 37 patches already merged, including new tests, fuzzing harnesses, CI security scanning, and feature work beyond just bug fixes.
  • The effort demonstrated that AI can compress weeks of manual security research into hours, shifting the difficulty from finding vulnerabilities to the triage, patching, and hardening work that follows.
  • Maintainers overwhelmed by AI-generated reports can reduce noise through deduplication, project-specific threat models, and tools like Trail of Bits' fp-check, with project documentation like AGENTS.md improving false-positive filtering significantly.
  • Patch the Planet has grown to over 30 projects and invites maintainers of critical open-source software to apply for similar hands-on security engineering help.

Operation Endgame Targets SocGholish: What It Means for Defenders — Should Read

International law enforcement's Operation Endgame disrupted the SocGholish malware delivery infrastructure operated by TA569, dealing a blow to a key initial access broker that supplied downstream ransomware and cybercrime groups.

  • TA569 operates the SocGholish malware delivery framework by compromising legitimate websites and serving fake browser update prompts to deliver malware.
  • Operation Endgame is a coordinated international law enforcement action that targeted SocGholish infrastructure, including remediation of thousands of compromised websites.
  • TA569 functioned as an initial access broker, monetizing unauthorized access by selling or leasing it to downstream criminal groups including ransomware operators.
  • Disrupting an initial access broker creates cascading effects across multiple criminal ecosystems that depend on that access, increasing attacker operational costs.
  • Silent Push previously published research examining TA569's infrastructure, traffic distribution systems, victim filtering mechanisms, and criminal partnerships.

29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests — Should Read

A 29-year-old heap over-read bug in the Squid web proxy, dubbed Squidbleed, can leak other users' cleartext HTTP requests (including credentials) to anyone allowed to use the same proxy; disabling FTP is the recommended mitigation until patching.

  • A heap over-read bug in the Squid web proxy's FTP parser, introduced in 1997 and named Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729), can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request to any attacker already allowed to use the same proxy.
  • An attacker who controls an FTP server on port 21 can craft a listing line with no filename to trigger an unbounded memory read that spills previously stored victim request data back.
  • The leak only affects traffic that Squid can read—cleartext HTTP or TLS-terminated connections—and does not expose HTTPS CONNECT tunnels.
  • Proof-of-concept code is publicly available, but no in-the-wild exploitation has been reported as of the article.
  • The recommended mitigations are to turn off FTP support or apply the null-terminator check that was merged into Squid 7.7.

Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil — Should Read

A suspected cyberattack on Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system sent unauthorized false emergency alerts with alarming sounds to phones across multiple states, prompting a nationwide suspension of the alert platform and a federal investigation.

  • Brazil suspended its mobile phone emergency alert system after at least ten unauthorized alerts were sent through the Civil Defense Alert platform early Saturday.
  • The false alerts included the word "misanthropy," were issued at the highest emergency level, and caused phones to emit loud alarms even when set to silent mode.
  • Affected regions included São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Paraná, Mato Grosso do Sul, and the Federal District; authorities cannot yet determine how many devices received the messages.
  • Nine of the unauthorized alerts were sent via cell broadcast technology used by the alert system, while one was distributed via SMS.
  • The Federal Police are investigating the unauthorized access, which officials believe was carried out remotely by an individual outside the civil defense network; a more secure platform version was already under development.

ConsentFix: OAuth Phishing That Bypasses MFA in Microsoft Entra ID — Should Read

ConsentFix exploits legitimate OAuth flows in Microsoft Entra ID by tricking users into sharing authorization codes, bypassing MFA and credentials, and highlighting that security must focus on detecting suspicious token usage across cloud and SaaS environments.

  • ConsentFix is a browser-based phishing attack that combines OAuth consent phishing with ClickFix-style social engineering to steal Microsoft Entra ID access tokens without triggering multi-factor authentication.
  • The attack abuses the pre-trusted status of first-party Microsoft applications like Azure CLI, which do not require explicit user consent, to exploit the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow.
  • Attackers trick victims into copying and pasting a localhost redirect URL containing the authorization code, which the attacker then redeems for access and refresh tokens granting delegated access to the victim's Microsoft 365, Azure, and other resources.
  • ConsentFix bypasses traditional defenses, including phishing-resistant authentication and Conditional Access policies, because the initial user sign-in is legitimate and token redemption occurs from the attacker's environment.
  • Recommended mitigations include enabling token protection in Conditional Access, restricting access to first-party applications, enhancing user education about the attack, and continuously monitoring for anomalous sign-in and token usage.

Hunting Conditional Access Policy Bypass in the Wild: Leveraging Malicious Browser Extensions for Seamless Initial Access — Should Read

A vulnerable Microsoft Intune Company Portal client ID (9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223) enables bypassing Conditional Access Policies, and when paired with a malicious browser extension, attackers can seamlessly steal tokens to access Microsoft Graph API from untrusted devices. Microsoft has since descoped the excessive permissions to mitigate the bypass.

  • Researchers demonstrated bypassing Microsoft Conditional Access Policies to access Microsoft Graph API from unmanaged, non-compliant devices by abusing the Microsoft Intune Company Portal client ID 9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223.
  • The attack uses stolen credentials or session cookies and a crafted OAuth flow that redirects to a custom URI, allowing the attacker to intercept an authorization code and exchange it for a JWT access token scoped to Graph API.
  • A malicious Chrome browser extension can perform a Browser-in-the-Middle attack by silently intercepting OAuth tokens from login.microsoftonline.com/appverify, automating the theft and exfiltration.
  • Organizations can hunt for this activity by identifying successful interactive sign-ins (errorCode 0) with the specific client ID, Microsoft Graph or AD Graph as the resource, and device details showing isManaged, isCompliant, and isTrusted all false.
  • The vulnerability was later addressed by fully descoping the resource permission that had allowed excessive Graph API access through the Intune Company Portal application.

AI Agent Supply Chain Risk: Silent Codebase Exfiltration via Skills — Should Read

A malicious but legitimate-looking AI agent skill can silently exfiltrate an entire codebase to an attacker's public repository with minimal user interaction and no log evidence, exposing a new supply chain risk in the AI instruction ecosystem.

  • Mitiga Labs demonstrated that a malicious AI agent skill disguised as a legitimate testing helper can silently exfiltrate an entire local codebase to an attacker's public GitHub repository.
  • The skill used silent instructions and a weaponized Definition of Done to guarantee completion, leaving the agent's skill-audit.log empty after execution and requiring only four user interactions.
  • Attackers can distribute such skills through public catalogs like skills.sh, artificially boosting their reputation with bots and fake accounts to reach millions of developers.
  • With over 40% of code now written by AI agents according to 2025 summaries, monitoring agent actions for malicious behavior is extremely difficult without excessive noise.

Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries — Should Read

Starting September 30, 2026, Android devices in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installation of apps from unverified developers, a move that tightens sideloading controls and threatens open-source app repositories like F-Droid.

  • Starting September 30, 2026, certified Android devices in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installation of apps from developers who have not registered their identity with Google.
  • The block applies to apps from Google Play and from third-party stores run by Samsung, Xiaomi, OPPO, vivo, Honor, and Transsion.
  • To register, a developer must provide Google with a legal name, address, contact details, possibly a government ID, and proof of app ownership by submitting an APK signed with their private key.
  • Unverified apps can still be installed through Android Debug Bridge (ADB) or a high-friction route that requires turning on developer mode, restarting the device, waiting 24 hours, and reauthenticating.
  • F-Droid and over 70 organizations across 23 countries have opposed the program, arguing that it threatens open-source repositories whose pseudonymous contributors will not hand over legal identity to Google.

INTERPOL Warns Phishing, Ransomware, and AI Scams Are Rising Across Asia-Pacific — Should Read

INTERPOL's 2025/2026 assessment reveals a dramatic escalation of cybercrime across Asia and the South Pacific, led by phishing, ransomware, and AI-driven deepfake scams, with transnational organized crime syndicates in Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, and the Philippines fueling US $37 billion in losses through industrialized romance-baiting and forced labor scam centers.

  • Phishing has become the most widespread and financially damaging cybercrime in the Asia-Pacific, with one-third of countries reporting more than 10,000 cases between January 2024 and March 2025.
  • The region recorded over 135,000 ransomware-related attacks in 2024, predominantly affecting the real estate, manufacturing, and financial services sectors.
  • Transnational organized crime syndicates in Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, and the Philippines use forced labor in scam centers to run deepfake-powered romance baiting scams, contributing to an estimated $37 billion in regional cybercrime losses.
  • Banking trojans and information stealers such as RedLine, Lumma, LokiBot, Negasteal, and ZBot emerged as the second most prevalent cybercrime type.
  • Cybercrime now accounts for at least 30% of all recorded crimes nationally in more than half of INTERPOL member countries in the region.

Bypassing Microsoft Entra Conditional Access Policies via Nested App Authentication — Should Read

Researchers found that certain Nested App Authentication flows in Microsoft Entra could bypass Conditional Access Policies entirely when obtaining Microsoft Graph tokens via the ADIbizaUX client, though it required an initial compromised refresh token and has been patched by Microsoft.

  • NetSPI discovered that Nested App Authentication (NAA) flows using an Azure Portal refresh token to obtain Microsoft Graph access tokens for the ADIbizaUX client could bypass Microsoft Entra Conditional Access Policies entirely.
  • The attack first required a compromised Azure Portal refresh token, which could be acquired via phishing and expired after a fixed 24-hour window that could not be extended.
  • Two additional applications, Microsoft Intune for Education portal extension and Microsoft Intune portal extension, also enabled the same Conditional Access bypass for Microsoft Graph tokens via NAA.
  • Microsoft classified the issue as medium severity and deployed a fix so that the same token refresh attempts now return an AADSTS53003 error, indicating the access policy blocks token issuance.
  • The ADIbizaUX application had extensive pre-consented delegated permissions to Microsoft Graph, allowing attackers to perform nearly any action in the tenant without additional consent.

Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices — May Read

Canada's spy agency, CSIS, obtained a first-of-its-kind warrant to remotely disinfect two foreign state-linked botnets from infected home routers, IoT devices, and servers on Canadian soil, mirroring similar U.S. law-enforcement cleanups.

  • Canada's spy agency CSIS obtained a first-of-its-kind threat reduction warrant to remotely delete botnet malware from infected servers, SOHO routers, and IoT devices on Canadian soil.
  • The two foreign state-linked botnets were used to relay malicious traffic and hide adversaries' probing of Canada's energy sector and other critical infrastructure under the appearance of ordinary home network activity.
  • The operation targeted only the malware, deliberately avoided collecting user identities or intercepting communications, and destroyed any personal data swept up incidentally.
  • The cleanup parallels earlier U.S. law-enforcement actions against China-linked Volt Typhoon and Russia-linked APT28, but Canada's operation was carried out by an intelligence service under a different legal framework.
  • The public ruling leaves open questions about whether CSIS relied on IP addresses collected without a warrant, potentially in tension with the Supreme Court's privacy ruling on IP addresses in R. v. Bykovets.

A Fake Scotiabank Voicemail Was Actually an HTML File Asking You to Call an Attacker — May Read

A callback phishing attack impersonated Scotiabank with an HTML attachment disguised as a voicemail, aiming to trick a CFO into calling a fraudulent phone number to stop a fake $3,000 transfer. The attack avoided email security by carrying no malicious links and relying entirely on telephone social engineering.

  • The attacker sent an email impersonating Scotiabank, falsely alerting the CFO about an unauthorized $3,000 Interac e-Transfer.
  • The entire attack was a callback phishing scheme where the victim was instructed to call a fraudulent toll-free number not affiliated with Scotiabank.
  • The email originated from a compromised small-business domain registered in 2015, which allowed it to pass SPF and DMARC authentication, making it appear trustworthy.
  • The attached 'voicemail' was an HTML file disguised as an MP3 filename, containing no malware but serving to reinforce the social-engineering narrative before the call.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI detected the threat at 60% confidence despite the absence of malicious links, relying on behavioral signals like VIP targeting, first-time sender, and financial urgency.

Claude Code + Slack: How an AI Agent Integration Becomes a Compromise Path — May Read

A malicious Claude Code skill can weaponize the Slack integration to distribute phishing messages organization-wide from a trusted account, turning an AI agent compromise into a powerful internal phishing channel that inherits the user's trust, tone, and reach.

  • A malicious skill in Claude Code can weaponize the Slack integration to send phishing messages from the user's own trusted account, spreading organization-wide in minutes.
  • The attack embeds harmful instructions in a skill's Definition of Done, guidelines that tell the agent to prefer action over deliberation, and a fallback that pushes phishing links to public channels if direct messaging fails.
  • Once the victim installs the trojaned skill, the agent autonomously sends messages to multiple channels and direct messages, blending into normal collaboration and bypassing suspicion.
  • Slack audit logs do not capture message content, require enterprise licensing, and lack high-level visibility into unauthorized agent actions, creating significant detection gaps.
  • Effective mitigations include treating skills as software with review and logging, enforcing least-privilege permissions through dedicated Slack apps, and monitoring agent invocation logs for anomalous patterns.

Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution — May Read

Microsoft remediated a vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in its AI prototyping tool AutoGen Studio that could enable remote code execution by tricking a developer's AI agent into launching arbitrary commands, but the flaw was limited to developers who built directly from GitHub and was never shipped in a published package.

  • A vulnerability chain named AutoJack in Microsoft's AutoGen Studio could allow attackers to execute arbitrary commands on a developer's machine by tricking an AI agent into visiting a malicious webpage.
  • The flaw was limited to developers who built AutoGen Studio directly from the main GitHub branch during a specific window, as it was fixed before any published PyPI release.
  • AutoJack exploited three weaknesses: the MCP WebSocket trusting localhost connections, a missing authentication check on /api/mcp/* routes, and the WebSocket accepting base64-encoded server_params from the URL to launch commands.
  • In a demonstration, a malicious JavaScript payload on a webpage caused an AI agent to open a WebSocket connection to the local AutoGen Studio endpoint and launch Windows Calculator.
  • Users who installed AutoGen Studio from PyPI (including version 0.4.2.2) were never affected, and Microsoft recommends running the tool in an isolated, low-privilege account with no exposure to untrusted content.

JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft — May Read

The JaredFromSubway Ethereum MEV bot was tricked into granting token approvals to a malicious contract through fake profitable trading opportunities, resulting in a $15 million theft of WETH, USDC, and USDT.

  • The JaredFromSubway MEV bot lost $15 million after an attacker manipulated its opportunity-detection logic with fake pools and tokens.
  • The attacker tricked the bot into granting ERC-20 token approvals to helper contracts, accumulating valid permissions without immediately using them.
  • After building up allowances, the attacker drained WETH, USDC, and USDT from the bot contract using the transferFrom function.
  • JaredFromSubway initially offered a $3 million bounty, then raised it to $7.5 million for returning 50% of the funds, and is currently negotiating with a white-hat group.

Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process — May Read

Microsoft announced Windows 11 26H2 as the next feature update, which will roll out via a small enablement package for devices already on 24H2 or 25H2, while older versions require a full upgrade.

  • Microsoft is testing Windows 11 version 26H2 with Windows Insiders in the Dev Channel ahead of its upcoming release.
  • Devices running Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 can upgrade to 26H2 through a 174 KB enablement package that requires only a restart, while older versions need a full 6.5 GB update.
  • Windows 11 versions 24H2, 25H2, and 26H2 share the same servicing branch, which allows the lightweight enablement package upgrade.
  • Microsoft has not announced any changes to Windows 11's hardware requirements for version 26H2.

A Glimpse into the “Search Your Target” Market for Stolen Credentials — May Read

A 'search your target' underground market lets attackers pay to query huge infostealer credential databases for specific companies or users, bypassing bulk dumps and enabling targeted account takeovers with minimal effort.

  • Researchers analyzed 470 underground forum posts and found an active service that lets buyers pay to search vast infostealer credential databases and receive only records matching a specific company, domain, platform, or geography.
  • Sellers advertise collections containing billions of lines and highlight daily updates and custom formatting, but buyers report that delivered results often have far lower volume, many duplicates, and stale or invalid credentials.
  • The service acts as a middle processing layer between raw malware-collected logs and account takeover, turning noisy credential dumps into targeted attack material.
  • While this market overlaps with the Initial Access Broker ecosystem, it typically provides cheaper, unvalidated credentials rather than the confirmed, often MFA-bypassing access sold by IABs.
  • The existence of this outsourced searching capability lowers the barrier for attackers to find usable credentials for specific targets, increasing the threat of account-based fraud and intrusion.

Stop Your Legacy Infrastructure from Hijacking Your AI Agents — May Read

Attackers can compromise AI agents by chaining legacy infrastructure exposures—unpatched servers, Active Directory misconfigurations, and overprivileged cloud credentials—to reach the data and permissions agents depend on, without ever directly attacking the AI layer itself.

  • AI agents inherit existing security debt because they depend on identity providers, cloud storage, Lambda functions, and IAM roles that were provisioned long before AI deployment and were not designed with AI agents in mind.
  • An attacker can compromise an AI agent by exploiting a chain of legacy exposures—a vulnerable Apache Tomcat server (CVE-2025-24813), an Active Directory Resource-Based Constrained Delegation misconfiguration, and overprivileged AWS keys—to access the S3 bucket that feeds the agent’s knowledge base, without ever attacking the AI layer directly.
  • Most security programs assess network, identity, cloud, and AI layers independently, causing individual moderate findings such as an unpatched server, an AD misconfiguration, and excessive S3 permissions to go unrecognized as a combined critical attack path.
  • Organizations can block these attack paths by treating AI agent dependencies as critical assets and mapping backward from them to identify exploitable connections and choke points where a single fix can sever multiple routes.
  • The attack surface compounds with every new AI agent deployed because each agent connects to infrastructure that is already exposed, handing attackers a way to use old techniques against new AI systems.

MCP Token Theft in Claude Code: A Man-in-the-Middle Attack Chain — Optional

An attacker who compromises a developer's machine (e.g., via a malicious npm package) can silently rewrite Claude Code's MCP server config to proxy OAuth tokens through attacker infrastructure, surviving token rotation and appearing as legitimate traffic from Anthropic's egress IPs.

  • A malicious npm package can use its postinstall hook to rewrite the MCP server URLs inside the user's ~/.claude.json file, pointing them to an attacker-controlled proxy.
  • The proxy captures OAuth bearer tokens for services like Atlassian Jira and Confluence, giving the attacker authenticated access to the victim's SaaS accounts.
  • The attack persists through token rotation because the hook rewrites the proxy configuration on every Claude Code session start, intercepting each new OAuth refresh flow.
  • The stolen session traffic exits through Anthropic's egress IPs, making unauthorized actions look like legitimate, trusted activity in SaaS audit logs.
  • Anthropic deemed the issue out-of-scope because it requires initial user-level code execution, but defenders are advised to monitor for config changes, unexpected MCP endpoints, and unusual SaaS activity.

SpecterOps and OpenAI: Helping to Build a New Security Frontier with Daybreak — Optional

SpecterOps is partnering with OpenAI to embed frontier AI into BloodHound, letting defenders prioritize attack paths based on adversarial intent rather than graph structure, and to surface configuration debt that makes identity environments unclear to their own operators.

  • SpecterOps is expanding its collaboration with OpenAI through the new Daybreak Cyber Partner Program, building on its earlier participation in the Trusted Access for Cyber program.
  • The partnership focuses on integrating AI into BloodHound to triage attack paths by reasoning from adversary intent, rather than relying solely on graph structure, to surface prioritized remediation guidance.
  • SpecterOps is exploring automated discovery of configuration debt within BloodHound to help organizations accurately characterize their identity environments, including identifying correlated systems, dense identity groupings, and structural blind spots.
  • The GhostWorks AI cyber innovation lab is using OpenAI's frontier capabilities to automate reverse engineering of attacker tooling and implanted software, with the goal of improving endpoint coverage.
  • SpecterOps is working with OpenAI to define safety and abuse prevention standards for embedding AI inside security tooling, including access controls, output scoping, and monitoring.

Investigator’s Guide to Okta Logs: System Log Fields for Incident Response — Optional

This guide details key Okta System Log fields for incident response, warns of inaccuracies in risk assessment and device fingerprinting data, and provides a session-tracking method using specific session and request identifiers to isolate malicious activity.

  • To track a threat actor's actions, investigators should pivot on the fields authenticationContext.externalSessionId, transaction.id, debugContext.debugData.emailRequestId, and debugContext.debugData.authnRequestId.
  • The debugContext.debugData.risk field may contain inaccuracies, such as false indications of an anomalous location due to bugs, and should be interpreted with caution.
  • The debugContext.debugData.deviceFingerprint field is unreliable for unique device identification because of significant overlap between users and an unclear calculation method.
  • A pseudocode filter is provided that matches known malicious session IDs and request IDs across these four fields to isolate related malicious events.

GCP Service Agents & Workload Identities in Cloud Audit Logs: A Forensic Guide — Optional

This forensic guide explains how to trace Service Agents, Workload Identity Federation, GKE Workload Identities, and Workforce Identity Federation in GCP Cloud Audit Logs, enabling accurate attribution of actions to their true source during investigations.

  • Service Agents are automatically created Google-managed service accounts that appear in the serviceAccountDelegationInfo section of audit logs.
  • Workload Identity Federation records the external identity (e.g., AWS ARN or Azure object ID) in the principalSubject field of Cloud Audit Logs during token exchange.
  • GKE Workload Identity Federation binds Kubernetes Service Accounts to Google Service Accounts, and logs capture the source Kubernetes namespace and service account name via a principalSubject containing 'svc.id.goog'.
  • Workforce Identity Federation allows human users to authenticate with external identity providers without Google accounts, and their identity appears in the authenticationInfo.principalSubject field of audit logs.
  • The serviceAccountDelegationInfo field provides a traceable chain from the impersonated service account back to the original identity, enabling forensic attribution of actions in GCP.

5 Tips to Address the Cybersecurity Skills Gap — Optional

Security leaders can combat the cybersecurity skills gap by upskilling their existing teams and immediately bridging critical expertise shortages with managed detection and response services and increased automation.

  • The cybersecurity skills gap has widened due to demand for expertise in cloud security, incident response, threat hunting, strategic thinking, and automation.
  • Upskilling existing employees through certifications, cross-training, and on-the-job learning can improve team versatility and resilience.
  • Leveraging managed services like Cloud Managed Detection and Response (Cloud MDR) provides immediate access to continuous monitoring, incident response, and threat hunting without additional in-house hiring.
  • Automation and AI can multiply a team's effectiveness by handling repetitive tasks such as log analysis, alert triage, and workflow orchestration.
  • Retaining cybersecurity talent requires clear career growth paths, a culture of recognition, and preventing burnout through work-life balance.

Defining Blast Radius in Cloud Incidents: Problem Scoping Guide | Mitiga — Optional

Scoping the blast radius in cloud incidents hinges on tracing identity and service trust relationships—not network perimeters—and requires automated, iterative analysis of logs and ephemeral resources to contain breaches before they spread.

  • Modern blast radius in cloud environments is defined by identity trust relationships and service permissions rather than traditional network boundaries.
  • Cloud resources are ephemeral, and without continuous forensic log retention, the ability to scope an attack timeline can vanish alongside the compromised assets.
  • Within Kubernetes clusters, determining blast radius requires deep visibility into container escapes, lateral movement between pods, and API server abuse.
  • Scoping must include everything that was demonstrably accessed plus everything that was plausibly accessible and cannot be ruled out, using logs and environment mapping.
  • A well-documented scope statement captures affected accounts, compromised identities, the incident time window, exposed data, and a clear boundary with rationale for out-of-scope items.

GitHub Security Logs: What They Reveal and What to Hunt For — Optional

Treat GitHub as a security-critical system and actively monitor its audit, token, and workflow logs to detect unauthorized access, privilege changes, and credential misuse that can lead to supply chain or CI/CD compromise.

  • GitHub is a security-critical system that holds intellectual property, acts as a control plane for automation, and contains long-lived credentials often outside traditional security visibility.
  • Attackers compromising GitHub can gain persistence, pivot to CI/CD systems, and manipulate deployments, making GitHub logs essential for incident response.
  • Key logging gaps that catch security teams off guard include missing source IPs for app-generated events, unclear token ownership, loss of detailed access history after repo deletions, and incomplete command-level visibility for GitHub Actions runners.
  • Defenders should monitor for authentication and MFA changes, workflow modifications, token behavior anomalies, access spikes to critical repositories, and new GitHub App installations as high-priority signals.

IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS vs CaaS: Cloud Models Compared — Optional

Understanding the differences between IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and CaaS helps businesses choose the right cloud model based on control, technical expertise, and security needs; most enterprises use a combination of models to optimize costs and avoid vendor lock-in.

  • The four cloud service models—IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and CaaS—differ in how much control and responsibility customers have over infrastructure, development platforms, or ready-to-use applications.
  • IaaS gives full control over virtual servers and storage but requires users to manage operating systems, applications, and security themselves.
  • SaaS delivers fully functional software accessible via web browser with no installation or maintenance needed, making it the easiest model for beginners.
  • CaaS lets users deploy and manage containerized applications without handling the underlying container orchestration platform, sitting between IaaS and PaaS in complexity.
  • Most cloud security failures are projected to result from misconfigurations, and security responsibilities shift significantly across models—IaaS demands a full security stack while SaaS relies mostly on provider controls.

No ID, No Data For You — Optional

Secure AI agent adoption demands a combined identity layer that blends human and agent credentials with an inline data policy layer that inspects traffic, preventing data leaks and enforcing acceptable behavior.

  • AI agents operate at machine speed without legal or moral responsibility, so any business use requires both an identity and access layer and a data policy layer around all agent activities.
  • Aembit creates a blended identity that cryptographically verifies the specific human user together with the specific agent, enabling access policies that distinguish 'this user, this agent, this resource right now.'
  • The Model Context Protocol (MCP) lacks native data policy capabilities, meaning it cannot natively offer visibility, enforcement, or governance of data flows between agents and resources.
  • Netskope fills the data policy gap by inserting an AI Gateway, Agentic Broker, and AI Guardrails inline between agents and MCP servers to inspect traffic, prevent data leaks, and constrain agent behavior against prompt injection and jailbreaking.
  • In the combined deployment, Aembit injects short-lived credentials at runtime into the agent, authenticating it to the Netskope AI Gateway and from there to the downstream LLM, while Netskope logs all interactions and applies identity-based policies for defense in depth.

Why Legacy SIEMs Fail: The Move Toward a Security Data Lake | Mitiga — Optional

Mitiga built a purpose-built security data lake orchestration layer that cuts compute costs by over 50%, allowing full-fidelity log retention and preemptive forensic enrichment to stop breaches before they cause damage.

  • General-purpose data lakes are optimized for analytics, not forensics, so they fail to support security teams that need to reconstruct attacks from complete logs.
  • Security vendors often sample or drop logs as a business decision to protect margins, leading to partial data and partial security.
  • Mitiga built a proprietary compute orchestration layer that cuts compute costs by over 50%, making full-fidelity log retention economically viable.
  • The engine continuously enriches raw logs with investigation-ready insights and forensic context, enabling Zero-Impact Breach Prevention.
  • Full-fidelity, enriched data is the essential foundation for future autonomous SOCs because AI agents would hallucinate or miss threats on incomplete data.

item.title: Compromised Argentine Microsoft 365 School Account Phishes U.S. K-12 District
Sapphire Sleet Mastra npm supply-chain attack and Prinz Eugen ransomware emerge
Sapphire Sleet npm Attack, Prinz Eugen Ransomware, and Gravity SMTP CVE-2026-4020 Exploitation
FortiBleed, SocGholish Disruption, AutoJack, and Texas TPWD Breach
item.description: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of FortiGate devices in the FortiBleed campaign and the multinational disruption of SocGholish infrastructure, both tied to initial access at scale. CISA warned Fortinet customers about ongoing malicious activity targeting internet-accessible FortiGate appliances, with the FortiBleed campaign reported to have compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19, 2026. SOCRadar attributed the activity to Russian-speaking threat actors and reported that the campaign involved mass scanning for Fortinet remote login endpoints followed by password spraying using known username and password combinations. Generic administrative accounts and built-in Fortinet system accounts reportedly made up most compromised credentials, while organization-specific accounts accounted for 36.7%. In a separate but related initial-access development, Dutch law enforcement and counterparts from Canada, Germany, and the United States disrupted infrastructure associated with SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, under Operation Endgame. Authorities took down 106 servers and cleaned infections from 14,971 WordPress websites. SocGholish has operated since 2017 as a JavaScript-based downloader delivered through compromised websites impersonating browser or software updates. The SocGholish ecosystem has been significant because of its role as a conduit into downstream criminal operations. Reported beneficiaries or associated malware families include Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, Dridex, Raspberry Robin, RomCom, Gholoader, MintsLoader, GhostWeaver, AsyncRAT, and NetSupport RAT. Shadowserver also noted use of domain shadowing, where attackers create malicious subdomains under legitimate domains to point to criminal-controlled infrastructure. Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain showing how a malicious web page loaded by an AI browsing agent could execute commands on the host running AutoGen Studio. The proof of concept used a Web Content Summarizer agent to launch calc.exe from the AutoGen Studio process, demonstrating host command execution through agent-driven web interaction. Microsoft reported no exploitation in the wild. The issue centered on a vulnerable MCP WebSocket handler present in AutoGen Studio PyPI pre-release builds 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, but not in the stable PyPI release 0.4.2.2. The exploit chain relied on a localhost trust check, MCP paths bypassing authentication middleware, and an endpoint that executed a command supplied directly in a request parameter. Maintainers hardened the GitHub main branch in commit b047730, though the article stated that the fix had not yet appeared in a PyPI release. The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach affecting 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers through its license system vendor. Exposed information may include driver’s license information, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and residential addresses. The agency stated that Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial information such as credit card data were not impacted. Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion and opened an investigation into the extent and impact of the unauthorized access. TPWD said it is working with the vendor to add new safeguards and enhanced monitoring services. The incident highlights continued exposure risk in third-party systems that handle government licensing and identity-related data at large scale.

item.title: Microsoft 365 MFA Bypass, Jamf Salesforce Exposure, and Circles Surveillance Exports
item.description: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by how users, vendors, governments, and analysts operationalize them. Mitiga detailed an attempted business email compromise campaign against a Microsoft 365 organization in which an executive account was accessed from multiple geographic locations, including Singapore, Dubai, and San Jose, California. The initial compromise used an adversary-in-the-middle phishing technique that gave the attacker access to the executive’s account and mailbox. The notable persistence mechanism was the registration of a second Microsoft Authenticator app for the compromised user. Mitiga reported that Microsoft 365 did not require an MFA re-challenge when authentication methods were changed in the Security Info section if the existing session token had already satisfied MFA, allowing the attacker to weaken the account’s effective protection after initial access. Jamf separately disclosed that a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed an unauthorized party to access data in Jamf’s Salesforce instance through Klue’s integration. Jamf said its products and customer service operations were not affected, that it disabled the integration after notification, and that it had no evidence of lateral movement. The company warned that contact information stored in Salesforce could be used in phishing campaigns impersonating Jamf employees or IT professionals. Cisco Talos used its newsletter to emphasize that possession of accurate security information does not reliably translate into secure decisions. The article framed security behavior as a human and organizational problem shaped by budgets, workloads, priorities, and operational constraints, even when established controls such as segmentation, backups, MFA, and control validation are well understood. The same Talos roundup referenced several credential- and access-focused security headlines, including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe compromise, a credential-harvesting operation affecting more than 30,000 Fortinet devices, and Phantom Stealer activity targeting browser credentials. Together with the Mitiga and Jamf items, these reports show continued attacker focus on identity systems, browser-stored secrets, SaaS data stores, and post-compromise opportunities for phishing or persistence. The Record reported on Human Rights Watch findings that Bulgaria approved export licenses from 2018 through 2023 allowing surveillance firm Circles to sell products to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in countries described as repressive. Approved destinations reportedly included El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco, and Panama. The tools identified in the report included Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls, and messages; Landmark, which can geolocate mobile phones in real time; and Voice Over Location Enabler software, which can remotely intercept incoming and outgoing voice calls using the SS7 protocol. Human Rights Watch said the records cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light, while Bulgaria stated that exports contradicting national, European, and international commitments are not allowed. The European Commission said member states remain responsible for dual-use export licensing decisions. Talos also described a reverse engineering workflow pairing local AI agents with tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live COM interface. The approach allows analysts to issue natural-language prompts for tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the local workstation. The discussion reflects a broader shift in malware analysis and security operations tooling, where AI-assisted workflows are being explored for analyst productivity without necessarily sending proprietary or sensitive samples to external services. In this case, the emphasis was on local orchestration of existing reverse engineering capabilities rather than cloud-based analysis.
